none: Two women lay dress jackets on a work table.

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: Nana (left) and Biddy (right) are played by Joan Emily Brown and Sue Clark, respectively. The two were not actresses but rather "volunteers at the Victoria & Albert Museum when Anderson, Costume Designer Mark Bridges and Day-Lewis were researching British couture during the summer of 2016. After helping the trio examine a Balenciaga original, Anderson discovered that both women had extensive backgrounds in fashion, with Brown having worked in two major houses on Savile Row during the 1950s. He hired both as creative consultants, based on their ability to verify in an instant whether a bobbin or pin was appropriate to the era, advising Melery as well in the details of the cutting room” (Burg, “Set Decore”). PTA speaks about this special dynamic of the film in “Academy Conversations: Phantom Thread.” For more on Bridges’s process of designing the costumes for Phantom Thread, see Syme, “Designing the Sumptuous Couture in Paul Thomas Anderson’s ‘Phantom Thread.’”

body.creator: Clara Smith
body.value: Catherine Join-Diéterle describes the studio environment of Cristóbal Balenciaga and how it was impacted by the designer’s personality as follows: “In Balenciaga’s studio the atmosphere that prevailed was one of concentration...and silence, which is probably the reason for the mystery that surrounds the man and his art. The importance of this has been exaggerated, however. Admittedly, silence attended his fashion shows, and his relations with the outside world, and unlike Dior, Balenciaga did not give interviews or lectures and refused to publish his life story. Explaining himself was alien to him; he loved life but for him, discretion was paramount” (Join-Diéterle, “Dior and Balenciaga,” 143). These details are clearly reflected in Phantom Thread.
